translatedText: William of Ockham was a 14th-century English Franciscan friar and theologian, considered one of the major figures in medieval intellectual history.
What is 'Ockham's razor'?

'Ockham's razor' is a principle stating that one should use the principle of parsimony when constructing explanations and theories for efficient reasoning, making a profound contribution to modern science.
Why was William of Ockham summoned to the Avignon Papacy?
Because his commentary on the Sentences was condemned as unorthodox at a synod in 1324, and he subsequently became embroiled in the controversy over apostolic poverty.
Was William of Ockham a nominalist?
William of Ockham is considered a pioneer of nominalism, arguing that only individuals exist and that universals are products of human mental operations.
Born in the Austrian Empire.
Authored The Science of Mechanics: A Critical and Historical Account of Its Development.
Authored the philosophical work The Analysis of Sensations.
Authored the philosophical work Knowledge and Error.
Died.
Development of the Critique of Mechanics
Developed a critique of mechanics by authoring The Science of Mechanics: A Critical and Historical Account of Its Development (1883).
Pioneering Independent Epistemology
Pioneered an independent epistemology from a positivist standpoint.
Research on Supersonic Jets and the 'Mach Number'
Conducted research on supersonic jets, and the 'Mach number' is named after him.
Influence on Einstein's Theory of Relativity
Influenced the formulation of Albert Einstein's theory of relativity by criticizing Newtonian mechanics in The Science of Mechanics (1883).
Advocacy of the Positivist Stance
Advocated a positivist stance in scientific epistemology, recognizing only what is given to the senses.
Proposal of the Unified Science Idea
Proposed the idea of unified science, arguing that all sciences deal with the same sensations and therefore agree at their foundation.
Starting Point of Logical Positivism
His positivism and unified science ideas became the starting point of modern logical positivism, which was initially named the Ernst Mach Society.
In what fields was Ernst Mach a scholar?
He was a physicist born in the Austrian Empire who lectured on the history and theory of science, developed a critique of mechanics, and pioneered an independent epistemology.
How is the 'Mach number' related to Ernst Mach?
The 'Mach number' is named after Ernst Mach, related to his research on supersonic jets and similar phenomena.
What was Ernst Mach's main philosophical position?

He advocated a positivist stance that recognized only what is given to the senses, and proposed the idea of a unified science, believing that all sciences align at their foundation because they deal with the same sensations.
What was the influence of Ernst Mach's ideas on later generations?
His critique of mechanics influenced Einstein's formulation of the theory of relativity, and his ideas on positivism and unified science became the starting point for modern logical positivism.
Born in Gloucestershire.
Entered the University of Oxford.
Received his Master of Arts degree.
Moved to Cambridge to study the Bible.
Completed the English New Testament based on the original Hebrew and Greek texts, and printed it in Germany.
Translated the Pentateuch in Hamburg.
Wrote An Answer unto Sir Thomas More's Dialogue, a rebuttal to Thomas More's A Dialogue Concerning Heresies.
Arrested in the Netherlands on charges of translating the Bible into English.
Early October 1536
Sentenced to death by imperial decree.
October 6, 1536
Burned at the stake as a heretic.
English Translation of the Koine Greek Bible
Influenced by John Wycliffe, he translated the Koine Greek Bible into English.
Creation of New Words
Created new words that did not previously exist for the purpose of the English translation.
Translation and Printing of the English New Testament Based on Original Hebrew and Greek Texts
Completed the English New Testament in 1526 through translation work based on original Hebrew and Greek texts and printed it in Germany.
Translation of the Pentateuch
Translated the Pentateuch in Hamburg in 1529.
Authorship of An Answer unto Sir Thomas More's Dialogue
Wrote an article refuting Thomas More's A Dialogue Concerning Heresies in 1531.
Laying the Foundation for the King James Bible
Seventy percent of the English version of the King James Bible is based on Tyndale's Bible.
Mastery of 7 Languages
Could speak seven languages, including English, Hebrew, Greek, Latin, Italian, Spanish, and French.
When was William Tyndale born?
William Tyndale was born in Gloucestershire in 1494.
What are the main achievements of William Tyndale?

He translated the Koine Greek Bible into English, and completed and printed the English New Testament based on the original Hebrew and Greek texts. He also laid the foundation for the King James Bible.
How did William Tyndale die?
He was arrested in 1535 on charges of translating the Bible into English and was burned at the stake as a heretic on October 6, 1536.
What languages could William Tyndale speak?
He could speak seven languages, including English, Hebrew, Greek, Latin, Italian, Spanish, and French.
What was William Tyndale's relationship with Thomas More?
William Tyndale harshly criticized Thomas More by writing An Answer unto Sir Thomas More's Dialogue in 1531, a rebuttal to More's A Dialogue Concerning Heresies.
His father, John Chaucer, was kidnapped by his aunt in an attempt to marry the 12-year-old boy to her daughter to keep the property in Ipswich.
Born in London as the son of a wealthy wine merchant.
His father, John Chaucer, married Agnes Copton, and inherited property including 24 shops from her uncle, Hamo de Copton.
First mentioned as a page to Elizabeth de Burgh, Countess of Ulster.
Traveled to France as a member of the English army with Elizabeth's husband, Lionel of Antwerp, during Edward III's invasion of France in the Hundred Years' War.
Captured and became a prisoner of war during the siege of Reims, and was released after Edward III paid a ransom of 16 pounds.
Married Philippa Roet.
October 25, 1400
Died.
Authorship of The Canterbury Tales
Best known as the author of the story collection The Canterbury Tales.
Known as the 'Father of English Literature' or 'Father of English Poetry'
Known as the first writer to write works in his native English rather than French or Latin.
Who was Geoffrey Chaucer?
He was an English author, poet, bureaucrat, courtier, and diplomat, best known as the author of the story collection The Canterbury Tales.
What is Geoffrey Chaucer's masterpiece?
His masterpiece is the unfinished The Canterbury Tales, a story collection influenced by Giovanni Boccaccio's Decameron.
Is there enough information about Geoffrey Chaucer's early life?
The original text states that very little information survives about Chaucer's early life and education.
Born in Dublin, Ireland, as a posthumous son of English parents.
Began to be active in politics and secured his position as a writer by publishing two satirical works anonymously.
Appointed as a clergyman but soon retired.
Died and was buried in St Patrick's Cathedral.
Start of Career as a Writer
Secured his position as a writer by publishing two satirical works anonymously in 1704.
Activity as a Political Commentator
Acted as a leading political commentator representing the ruling Tory party at the time.
Authorship of Gulliver's Travels
Wrote Gulliver's Travels, considered the most outstanding satirical novel that sharply criticized human beings and society.
Earning the Title of Irish Patriot
Earned the title of 'Irish Patriot' by writing articles criticizing Britain's responsibility for the starvation and poverty in Ireland and advocating for Irish independence.
What was Jonathan Swift's profession?
He was an Anglo-Irish novelist and Anglican cleric.
What is Jonathan Swift's representative literary work?
His representative work is Gulliver's Travels, considered the greatest satirical novel that sharply criticized human nature and society.
How was Jonathan Swift evaluated in Ireland?
He earned the title of 'Irish Patriot' for writing works that blamed Britain for Ireland's famine and poverty and advocated for Ireland's self-reliance.
July 1, 1804
Born in Paris.
Lost her father at the age of 4 and was raised by her grandmother.
December 10, 1822
Married Baron Dudevant, a provincial aristocrat.
Left home with her two children and moved to Paris.
Rose to fame upon publishing the serial novel Indiana.
Published the autobiographical romance novel Lélia.
Published the humanitarian social novel Consuelo.
Published the pastoral novel La Mare au Diable (The Devil's Pool).
Published François le Champi (The Country Waif).
